content:
# Romans Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 4 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 4:1 — Structure and subject of the opening question

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What then shall we say that Abraham the forefather of us found according to [the] flesh?" | Literal — "found" (heurekeni) is the main verb; "according to the flesh" modifies "found" or "forefather" |
| LOGOS | "What then shall we say was gained by Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh?" | Formal — "gained" for heurekeni; "according to the flesh" modifies "forefather" |
| PNEUMA | "Abraham is the father of our Jewish nation. What did he discover about being made right with God?" | Dynamic — splits into two sentences; interprets "found" as "discover" about justification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The syntactical question (does "according to the flesh" modify "forefather" or "found"?) is resolved by linking it with "forefather," which is the majority scholarly reading. PNEUMA's restructuring loses the single rhetorical question and adds "being made right with God" — an interpretation of the question, not its content.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:3 — "counted" vs. "reckoned" vs. "counted"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it was reckoned to him for righteousness" | "Reckoned" — traditional rendering of elogisthe; preserves the accounting metaphor |
| LOGOS | "it was counted to him as righteousness" | "Counted" — same accounting metaphor with more accessible language |
| PNEUMA | "God counted him as righteous because of his faith" | Dynamic — makes God the explicit subject; adds "because of his faith" as causal explanation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "counted." The verb logizomai (count/reckon/credit) is the key word of the chapter, appearing 11 times. "Counted" is clear and maintains the accounting metaphor. PNEUMA's causal "because of" shifts the logic — Paul says faith was counted as righteousness, not that righteousness was given because of faith (a subtle but important distinction).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The logizomai terminology is standardized as "counted" throughout the chapter.

---

## 4:4 — "wages" vs. "wage" — economic metaphor

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the [one] working, the wage is not reckoned according to grace but according to debt" | Literal — misthos rendered as "wage"; "according to debt" (kata opheilema) |
| LOGOS | "to the one who works, his wages are not counted as a gift but as his due" | "Wages" / "gift" / "due" — varied vocabulary for the economic contrast |
| PNEUMA | "When people work, their wages are not a gift, but something they have earned" | Dynamic — "earned" replaces "due/debt"; makes the analogy more accessible |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The wage/gift contrast is central. LOGOS captures both poles clearly. PNEUMA's "earned" is accurate but loses the specific Greek vocabulary of obligation (opheilema = "debt/obligation").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:5 — "justifies the ungodly" vs. "forgives sinners"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "believing upon the [one] justifying the ungodly" | Literal — ton dikaiounta ton asebe |
| LOGOS | "believes in him who justifies the ungodly" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "because of their faith in God who forgives sinners" | Dynamic — "forgives sinners" replaces "justifies the ungodly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Justifies the ungodly" is a critical Pauline phrase that PNEUMA fundamentally alters. "Forgives" is not the same as "justifies" — justification is a forensic declaration of righteousness, not merely pardon. And "sinners" is not the same as "ungodly" (asebeis) — asebeis specifically denotes those without piety/reverence for God. This is one of PNEUMA's most significant theological losses in the chapter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's rendering is flagged as theologically inadequate.

---

## 4:7-8 — Psalm 32 quotation: "blessed" vs. "joy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Blessed [are those] whose lawless deeds were forgiven and whose sins were covered; blessed [is the] man to whom the Lord will not at all reckon sin" | Literal — makarioi rendered as "blessed" |
| LOGOS | "Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, and whose sins are covered; blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Oh, what joy for those whose disobedience is forgiven, whose sins are put out of sight! Yes, what joy for those whose record the Lord has cleared of sin!" | Dynamic — "joy" for makarioi; "put out of sight" for "covered"; "record cleared" for "not count" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Blessed" is the standard translation of makarioi and preserves the beatitude form. PNEUMA's "joy" captures the emotional register but loses the formal declaration. "Put out of sight" and "record cleared" are interpretive expansions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:11 — "sign" and "seal" — covenant terminology

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he received [the] sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith, the [one] in the uncircumcision" | Literal — preserves the complex genitive chain |
| LOGOS | "He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised" | Formal — unpacks the genitives with relative clauses |
| PNEUMA | "He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness he had already received by faith while still uncircumcised" | Dynamic — adds "already received" to emphasize the temporal priority of faith |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's addition of "already received" is technically correct (the temporal argument is Paul's point) but adds a word not in the Greek. LOGOS's "that he had by faith" communicates the same temporal priority without addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:13 — "heir of the world" vs. "give the whole earth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the promise to Abraham or to the seed of him — [that] he [would be] heir of [the] world" | Literal — kleronomos tou kosmou |
| LOGOS | "the promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "God's promise to give the whole earth to Abraham and his descendants" | Dynamic — "give the whole earth" for "heir of the world"; makes God the explicit agent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Heir of the world" is Paul's language — it goes beyond the land promise of Genesis to a cosmic inheritance. PNEUMA's "whole earth" narrows the scope (kosmos = world/universe, not just earth/land).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:15 — Parenthetical on law and transgression

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For the law produces wrath; but where there is no law, neither [is there] transgression" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "For the law brings wrath, but where there is no law there is no transgression" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "For the law always brings punishment on those who try to obey it. (The only way to avoid breaking the law is to have no law to break!)" | Dynamic — adds interpretive parenthetical; "punishment" for "wrath"; "try to obey" is not in the text |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's parenthetical "(The only way to avoid breaking the law is to have no law to break!)" is a clever reading but overly interpretive. "Punishment" also narrows "wrath" (orge), which in Paul has eschatological dimensions beyond mere punishment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:17 — "calls into existence the things that do not exist" vs. "creates new things out of nothing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "calling the [things] not being as being" | Literal — kalountos ta me onta hos onta |
| LOGOS | "calls into existence the things that do not exist" | Formal — "calls into existence" renders the creative speech act |
| PNEUMA | "creates new things out of nothing" | Dynamic — "creates out of nothing" (creatio ex nihilo language) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul's language is about divine creative speech — God "calls" (kalountos) non-existent things into being. LOGOS preserves the speech-act dimension. PNEUMA's "creates out of nothing" is theologically accurate but loses the "calling" metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:18 — "In hope against hope" vs. "Even when there was no reason for hope"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Who against hope, upon hope believed" | Literal — par' elpida ep' elpidi |
| LOGOS | "In hope against hope he believed" | Formal — preserves the paradoxical construction |
| PNEUMA | "Even when there was no reason for hope, Abraham kept hoping" | Dynamic — resolves the paradox into a temporal narrative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "In hope against hope" preserves the deliberate paradox — Abraham's hope existed in the absence of hope. PNEUMA's rendering dissolves the paradox into a chronological sequence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:19 — Sinaiticus variant: "he did not consider" vs. "he considered"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he considered the body of himself already having been deadened" | Notes the Sinaiticus reading ou katenoesen (with negative) but renders without it, following some manuscript traditions |
| LOGOS | "He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body" | Follows a reading where Abraham did consider the evidence — his faith was not ignorant of the facts |
| PNEUMA | "Abraham's faith did not weaken, even though, at about 100 years of age, he figured his body was as good as dead" | Dynamic — "figured" for katenoesen; follows reading where Abraham acknowledged the evidence |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which preserves the Sinaiticus tradition that Abraham did consider the evidence. Faith is not denial of reality but trust that transcends it. The council notes document the variant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Textual variant noted.

---

## 4:19 — "barrenness of Sarah's womb" vs. "deadness of the womb"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the deadness of the womb of Sarah" | Literal — nekrosin tes metras Sarras |
| LOGOS | "the barrenness of Sarah's womb" | Formal — "barrenness" interprets nekrosis (deadness) functionally |
| PNEUMA | "and so was Sarah's womb" | Dynamic — elliptical ("as good as dead... and so was Sarah's womb") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. While EMET's "deadness" is lexically precise (nekrosis = deadness/death), "barrenness" communicates the functional meaning — Sarah's womb was dead in the sense that it could not produce life.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:20 — "grew strong" vs. "was empowered" vs. "grew stronger"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was empowered by faith" | Literal — enedunamothe, passive: "was empowered/strengthened" |
| LOGOS | "he grew strong in his faith" | Formal — renders the passive as an intransitive growth |
| PNEUMA | "his faith grew stronger" | Dynamic — makes "faith" the subject of growth |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET correctly identifies the passive voice (God is the implied agent empowering Abraham), but "grew strong" communicates the result naturally. PNEUMA's shift of subject from Abraham to "his faith" is a subtle but real change.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, with EMET's passive observation noted.

---

## 4:25 — "delivered up" vs. "handed over" — paradidomi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who was handed over because of the trespasses of us, and was raised because of the justification of us" | Literal — paredothe / egerthe; "handed over" for paradidomi |
| LOGOS | "who was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification" | Formal — "delivered up" for paradidomi |
| PNEUMA | "He was handed over to die because of our sins, and he was raised to life to make us right with God" | Dynamic — adds "to die" and "to life"; "make us right" for dikaiosis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Delivered up" has stronger creedal resonance than "handed over" and matches traditional Christian confession language. PNEUMA's additions ("to die," "to life") are interpretive expansions — the Greek says only "delivered" and "raised."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET too literal / wooden for English | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA too expansive / interpretive | 10 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS or EMET |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA nearly identical | 11 | LOGOS rendering adopted as consensus baseline |
| Key theological term at stake | 2 | "Justifies the ungodly" (v.5); logizomai rendering (v.3) |
| Textual variant | 1 | v.19 — Sinaiticus negative noted |
| Preservation of literary features | 2 | "In hope against hope" (v.18); creedal formula (v.25) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 4 is Paul's scriptural argument from Abraham, and the translation divergences center on two issues: (1) the consistent rendering of the key theological verb logizomai ("count/reckon"), and (2) how dynamically to render Paul's dense argumentative prose.

EMET's greatest contribution is the preservation of logizomai's accounting metaphor ("reckoned") and the passive voice in v.20 ("was empowered"), which points to divine agency. LOGOS provides the most balanced renderings throughout, particularly in preserving the paradox of "hope against hope" (v.18) and the creedal formula of v.25. PNEUMA's most significant theological failure in this chapter is the rendering of v.5, where "forgives sinners" replaces "justifies the ungodly" — a substitution that loses the forensic and scandalous dimensions of Paul's claim.

The chapter's consistent use of logizomai (11 occurrences) required a uniform translation decision: the consensus standardized on "counted" throughout, balancing accessibility with the accounting metaphor.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
